title: Arterial Line Damping, Zeroing and Levelling

abridged.title: Abridged Snapshot
abridged.content:
Bedside snapshot

Damping, zeroing and levelling decide whether the arterial pressure number is trustworthy. In CT-ICU, an inaccurate arterial line can drive harmful treatment: unnecessary vasodilators for a falsely high systolic pressure, missed hypotension from overdamping, or inappropriate fluid/vasopressor changes after cardiac surgery.

Dynamic response — The ability of the arterial monitoring system to reproduce the patient’s real arterial pressure without excessive blunting or ringing.

Overdamping — A sluggish system that blunts the waveform. It underestimates systolic pressure, overestimates diastolic pressure and may make pulse pressure falsely narrow.

Underdamping — An oscillating system that rings after pressure changes. It overestimates systolic pressure, underestimates diastolic pressure and creates a falsely wide pulse pressure.

Problem Typical appearance Immediate action
Overdamping Flattened upstroke, absent notch, narrow pulse pressure. Check for clot, air, kink, loose connection, low flush pressure, catheter against wall; flush and square-wave test.
Underdamping Tall sharp systolic peak, oscillations after flush, wide pulse pressure. Remove excess tubing, secure limb, remove air, tighten connections, avoid soft/compliant tubing.
Incorrect zero All values offset high or low. Open stopcock to air, close to patient, press zero, then return stopcock to patient monitoring position.
Incorrect level Pressure wrong by height difference. Place transducer at right atrial/phlebostatic level.

A transducer 10 cm below the right atrium can over-read pressure by about 7.4 mmHg; 10 cm above can under-read by about 7.4 mmHg. This is enough to change vasopressor decisions.

expanded.title: Expanded Deep-Dive
expanded.content:
Why damping matters

The arterial system is a dynamic pressure system. The catheter and tubing must transmit pressure rapidly and accurately to the transducer. If the system is too compliant, partially blocked or full of air, the pressure wave is absorbed and blunted. If the system resonates, the pressure wave overshoots and rings. Both errors can produce numbers that look precise but are physiologically wrong.

Overdamping is dangerous in CT-ICU because it can hide hypertension and make pulse pressure appear narrow. Underdamping is dangerous because it can exaggerate systolic hypertension after aortic surgery or valve surgery, leading to unnecessary vasodilators or confusion about bleeding risk. MAP is generally less affected than systolic or diastolic pressure, but severe damping errors can distort all values.

Fast flush test — A dynamic response test where the flush device is briefly activated to create a square wave, then released to observe oscillations. It helps distinguish optimal damping, overdamping and underdamping.

Optimal square wave response

A good response has a rapid vertical rise, flat plateau, rapid fall and usually 1 to 2 oscillations before returning to baseline. This means the system is fast enough to transmit the waveform without excessive ringing.

Overdamped response

An overdamped response has slow rise/fall and little or no oscillation. Causes include clot at catheter tip, air bubbles, kinked tubing, loose connection, blood in tubing, catheter against vessel wall, low pressure bag, soft tubing or long extension tubing.

Underdamped response

An underdamped response has excessive oscillations, often more than 2 after the flush. Causes include long tubing, overly stiff or resonant setup, catheter whip, patient movement and poor limb positioning. The displayed systolic pressure can be falsely high by 10-30 mmHg or more in severe cases.

Phlebostatic axis — The approximate right atrial level, usually the fourth intercostal space at the mid-axillary line in the supine patient. It is the standard reference level for arterial, CVP and PA pressures.

Hydrostatic pressure changes by approximately 0.74 mmHg per cm of height difference between transducer and the reference level. If the transducer is too low, the column of fluid adds pressure and the reading is falsely high. If it is too high, the reading is falsely low. For a patient sitting up, level to the right atrium, not the mattress or bed frame.

pearls.title: Clinical Pearls
pearls.content:
If the waveform is overdamped, systolic pressure may be falsely low. This can make a hypertensive aortic surgery patient look safe when they are not.

If the waveform is underdamped, do not chase the systolic pressure blindly. Check MAP, square-wave response and clinical context.

Levelling errors are common after bed height changes, sitting the patient up, turning, mobilising, imaging transfers and return from theatre.

Zeroing is not the same as levelling. Zeroing calibrates atmospheric pressure; levelling aligns the transducer to the patient’s right atrium. You need both.

equipment.title: Equipment Tie-In
equipment.content:
Nurse-technician setup

Troubleshooting is equipment plus physiology. If the line is technically poor but the patient is unstable, escalate while troubleshooting. Do not spend ten minutes perfecting a trace while a tamponade, bleed or RV failure is evolving.

targets.title: Targets and Numbers
targets.content:
Parameter Usual target/range CT-ICU interpretation
MAP 65-80 mmHg for most stable post-cardiac surgery patients; 70-90 mmHg often used when renal, cerebral, aortic or graft perfusion is a concern. Interpret with urine output, lactate, mentation, skin perfusion and surgical target. A MAP of 65 can be inadequate in chronic hypertension or after aortic/neurological risk surgery.
Pulse pressure Usually 30-50 mmHg. Narrow pulse pressure suggests low stroke volume, tamponade, severe vasoconstriction or RV failure. Very wide pulse pressure suggests low SVR, aortic regurgitation or IABP augmentation.
Urine output At least 0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly aim 30 mL/hr minimum in average adult. Falling urine output with a deteriorating waveform is an early perfusion warning after cardiac surgery.
Lactate Usually <2 mmol/L; trend should fall after bypass and resuscitation. Persistent or rising lactate >3-4 mmol/L suggests ongoing oxygen debt, low cardiac output, vasoplegia, bleeding, sepsis or limb/mesenteric ischaemia.
Technical number Value Meaning
Hydrostatic error About 0.74 mmHg per cm height difference 10 cm error equals about 7.4 mmHg BP error.
Pressure bag 300 mmHg Maintains continuous flush and prevents blood reflux.
Optimal flush test 1-2 oscillations Suggests acceptable dynamic response.
Overdamped flush test No oscillation or slow return Blunted trace; check clot/air/kink/flush/catheter position.
Underdamped flush test >2 oscillations/ringing Exaggerated systolic and low diastolic; secure/shorten system and remove air.

quiz.question: A transducer is 10 cm below the phlebostatic axis. What will happen?
options: BP will read falsely high by about 7-8 mmHg
BP will read falsely low by about 7-8 mmHg
quiz.explanation: A transducer below the right atrium adds hydrostatic pressure. Zeroing does not correct levelling error.

quiz.question: A fast flush test shows no oscillation and a slow return to baseline. What is this?
quiz.explanation: A sluggish square wave response with no oscillation indicates overdamping, which blunts systolic pressure and narrows pulse pressure.

quiz.question: Which value is usually least distorted by mild underdamping?
quiz.explanation: MAP is generally less affected than systolic and diastolic values in mild-moderate damping problems, though severe technical faults can affect all readings.
